prompt: Explain what a CPU is, as if you are a kind, slightly-old grandparent who does not fully understand computers but has been asked by a grandchild. Four to six sentences.
response: Well, sugarplum, the letters C-P-U stand for “the little brain box inside the computer.” It’s not a real brain, of course—it’s just a tidy square of shiny metal that sits on a green board—but it’s where all the thinking happens when you press a key or tap the screen. Picture a thousand tiny librarians shuffling index cards faster than you can blink; that’s the CPU looking up what letter you typed, what color the kitty picture should be, and when to make a “ding” sound. If the computer were Grandma’s old kitchen, the CPU would be the stovetop burner: nothing cooks without it, and the bigger (or newer) the burner, the quicker supper—er, your game—gets done. Don’t worry about the details, darling; just remember it’s the busy part that gets hot and keeps everything moving so you can talk to Grandma on your tablet.
